insights.insight: Verified purchase receipts settle at ~$0.10 per conversion event versus $5–$150 CPA for probabilistic ad tech attribution — a 50–200x cost differential driven by eliminating the attribution problem entirely, not optimizing around it.

With Chrome completing cookie deprecation and device fingerprinting increasingly blocked by regulation and browser privacy updates, the industry has no cross-ecosystem deterministic signal at scale — receipt-layer data is structurally positioned to fill that gap.

Google, Apple, Microsoft, and Stripe each capture purchase signals only within their own ecosystems and tied to identity; a receipt-based protocol aggregates across all payment rails without identity attachment, producing the cross-ecosystem view no single platform can sell.

Verified receipt signals are now reaching rural and secondary markets across the Northeast — exactly where probabilistic targeting has the least accuracy and ad tech data quality is weakest, making these signals proportionally more valuable for brands with broad geographic distribution.

Fetch and Ibotta hold real purchase data but it is identity-attached and not queryable by third parties; the structural difference here is a protocol that produces verified spend signals without identity linkage, which changes the privacy liability calculus for brands using the data.

Five pending vendor approvals — Notion, Render, Vercel, DigitalOcean, Hetzner — signal SaaS subscription receipts entering the dataset; for B2B marketers, verified SaaS spend is a high-intent, high-predictability signal category that does not currently exist in any open data market.
